bibliographic_data.title: R2T: Rule-Encoded Loss Functions for Low-Resource Sequence Tagging
bibliographic_data.abstract: We introduce the Rule-to-Tag (R2T) framework, a hybrid approach that integrates a multi-tiered system of linguistic rules directly into a neural network’s training objective. R2T’s novelty lies in its adaptive loss function, which includes a regularization term that teaches the model to handle out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words with principled uncertainty. We frame this work as a case study in a paradigm we call principled learning (PrL), where models are trained with explicit task constraints rather than on labeled examples alone. Our experiments on Zarma part-of-speech (POS) tagging show that the R2T-BiLSTM model, trained only on unlabeled text, achieves 98.2% accuracy, outperforming baselines like AfriBERTa fine-tuned on 300 labeled sentences. We further show that for more complex tasks like named entity recognition (NER), R2T serves as a powerful pre-training step; a model pre-trained with R2T and fine-tuned on just 50 labeled sentences outperforms a baseline trained on 300.

core_content.novelty_claim: The R2T framework integrates linguistic rules directly into the neural network’s training objective, providing an adaptive out-of-vocabulary (OOV) loss term that teaches principled uncertainty.
core_content.key_methodology: R2T uses a multi-tiered rule system and an adaptive loss function to guide model learning. It combines pre-trained word embeddings with character-level representations for handling OOV words.
outcomes: The R2T-BiLSTM model achieves 98.2% accuracy on Zarma POS tagging without labeled data, outperforming a baseline fine-tuned on 300 sentences.
For NER tasks, pre-training with R2T and fine-tuning on just 50 labeled sentences outperforms a baseline trained on 300.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The Rule-to-Tag (R2T) framework is introduced as a hybrid approach that integrates linguistic rules directly into the neural network’s training objective. This method, combined with an adaptive loss function, teaches models to handle out-of-vocabulary words with principled uncertainty. The R2T model, trained on unlabeled text, achieves 98.2% accuracy in Zarma POS tagging, surpassing a baseline fine-tuned on 300 labeled sentences. For more complex tasks like named entity recognition (NER), pre-training with R2T and fine-tuning on just 50 labeled sentences outperforms a baseline trained on 300.
